label: What chain analysis firms actually do
brief:
Chain analysis is mostly heuristics plus subpoenas, not cryptography. The structural part — running the heuristics over the public chain — produces clusters of addresses that probably belong to one entity. The two big ones are common-input-ownership (multiple inputs in one transaction belong to the same wallet) and change-output identification (one of the outputs of a typical user-payment looks like change going back to the sender) [1]. Both are statistical guesses that fail the moment a coinjoin, payjoin, or unusual transaction shape gets introduced.

The non-structural part — turning a cluster into a name — comes from the regulated edges of the system. Exchanges and brokers do KYC at sign-up and report deposits and withdrawals on subpoena; surveillance vendors stitch their own clusters to those names and resell the stitched dataset to other exchanges, banks, and law enforcement [2]. This is also why "chain analysis can de-anonymize Bitcoin" claims should be read carefully: they conflate "we have a database of exchange-supplied identities tagged to known clusters" with "we cracked the chain." The first is true and dangerous if you cycle coins through KYC venues; the second is not happening.

Where the marketing diverges from reality: aggregate cluster sizes get reported with confidence intervals that are not always disclosed; coins that have passed through coinjoins are sometimes labeled "high risk" by default rather than analyzed; and the chain itself doesn't distinguish a collaborative spend from a 1-input-1-output transfer to yourself [3]. The defensive posture for an individual is the same regardless: avoid address reuse, separate the UTXOs that touch KYC from the UTXOs that don't, and use coinjoin or payjoin when you need to break the linkage between two histories.

sources.label: Sarah Meiklejohn et al. — A Fistful of Bitcoins (2013)
sources.excerpt: We use the heuristic that all input addresses in a single transaction are controlled by the same user. Combined with a small number of identified addresses obtained by interacting with services, this lets us cluster a substantial fraction of the active address set into entities. Most subsequent commercial chain analysis is a refinement of this approach.

sources.label: Bitcoin Optech — Privacy improvements topic
sources.excerpt: Practical Bitcoin privacy is a question of which heuristics are usable against your wallet's transaction history. The heuristics depend on patterns that are easy to introduce by accident — round-number outputs, address reuse, change in a different script type — and easy to break with intent: payjoin, coinjoin, or careful UTXO management.

sources.label: Matt Corallo — On chain surveillance and what it can and can't do (bitcoin-dev list)
sources.excerpt: Most of what is sold as 'chain analysis' is the combination of public-chain heuristics with private databases of exchange-supplied identities. The chain itself does not authenticate identity. The risk model is not 'they will deanonymize my wallet from the chain'; it is 'they already know which wallet is mine because of where I bought the coins, and now they can follow it.'
